questions.prompt: Two algorithms are both O(n): one scans an array sequentially, the other traverses a linked list of the same length. Why can the array version run several times faster in practice despite identical Big-O?

options.text: Array elements are contiguous, so the CPU's cache prefetcher loads upcoming elements before they're requested; linked-list nodes can live at scattered heap addresses, causing cache misses on each dereference.
Big-O notation is only an approximation and is frequently wrong for O(n) algorithms.
Linked lists always have a higher constant factor because they store two fields (value and pointer) per node.
Arrays are always allocated on the stack, which is faster than heap memory.

questions.explanation: Big-O counts operations, not memory latency. Contiguous memory lets hardware prefetching hide most access latency; pointer-chasing defeats prefetching because the next address isn't known until the current node is read. (b) Big-O is exact for operation counts, not a wrong approximation. (c) extra field storage doesn't explain the access-pattern cost. (d) arrays are commonly heap-allocated too (e.g. `new int[n]`); the win is contiguity, not stack-vs-heap.

questions.prompt: What is the precise, defensible claim about `ArrayList.add`'s time complexity?

options.text: It is amortized O(1): most calls are O(1), but any individual call may be O(n) when it triggers a backing-array resize.
It is O(1) in the worst case, because Java pre-allocates enough capacity for any list.
It is O(log n) because resizing uses a doubling strategy.
It is O(n) because every add() copies the whole array to check for space.

questions.explanation: The amortized analysis: across n inserts, total copying work sums to a geometric series ≈ 2n, so average cost per insert is O(1) — but a specific call that triggers the resize does O(n) work copying every existing element. (b) there's no such universal pre-allocation. (c) confuses the number of resizes (O(log n) total resizes) with per-call complexity. (d) overstates — most calls do NOT copy the array, only resize-triggering ones do.

questions.prompt: A teammate wrote this to build a large CSV row from many field values. What's the performance flaw and the fix?

options.text: Each `+` allocates a new string and copies both operands, making total cost O(n^2) in the number of fields; use a StringBuilder to make it O(n).
The trailing comma is a bug that will break CSV parsing.
String concatenation in Java always uses StringBuilder internally, so there is no performance issue here.
The loop should use fields.stream().forEach() instead of a for-each loop for better performance.

questions.explanation: Because strings are immutable, `row + f + ","` builds an entirely new string each iteration, copying everything accumulated so far — classic O(n^2) behavior for n fields. Replacing with `StringBuilder` (append in a loop, `toString()` once) makes it O(n) total. (b) is a real but separate issue, not a performance flaw. (c) is a common misconception — javac only auto-optimizes concatenation within a *single* expression/statement, not across loop iterations reassigning a variable. (d) stream/forEach doesn't change the underlying string-allocation cost at all.

questions.prompt: A dynamic array implementation grows its capacity by a fixed amount (+16) rather than doubling. What is the consequence for amortized insert cost?

options.text: Amortized cost degrades to O(n) per insert, because resizes now happen O(n) times instead of O(log n) times, and each resize still copies O(n) elements.
There is no consequence; amortized cost is always O(1) regardless of growth strategy.
Memory usage becomes worse than doubling, since fixed growth always over-allocates more than doubling.
Insert cost becomes O(log n) because the growth is linear instead of exponential.

questions.explanation: With +16 growth, reaching size n requires roughly n/16 resizes (O(n) resizes total), each copying up to n elements — total copying work becomes O(n^2)/16, i.e. O(n^2), making amortized cost per insert O(n), not O(1). This is precisely why standard libraries use a multiplicative growth factor. (b) is false — growth strategy is exactly what determines the amortized bound. (c) is backwards: fixed growth is memory-tight (the actual trade-off this chapter names), it's the time cost that suffers. (d) misstates the resulting complexity class.

questions.prompt: Which problem is the best fit for a two-pointer or sliding-window approach rather than a nested loop?

options.text: Finding the longest substring without repeating characters, where the window's left edge only ever needs to move forward as the right edge advances.
Finding all pairs of elements in an unsorted array that sum to a target, where every pair must be checked independently.
Sorting an array in place.
Computing the transpose of a 2D matrix.

questions.explanation: The defining signal for two-pointer/sliding-window is monotonic movement: once a character repeats, the window's left edge advances and never needs to move backward, so total pointer movement across the whole scan is O(n) rather than O(n^2). (b) as stated (unsorted, all pairs) has no monotonic structure to exploit directly (though sorting first + two pointers is a different, valid technique) — checking every pair really is the naive shape here. (c) and (d) aren't two-pointer problems; they're different traversal patterns entirely.

questions.prompt: Why do modern standard libraries (Java ArrayList, C++ std::vector, Python list) default to array-backed dynamic arrays rather than linked lists, even for workloads with frequent insertions?

options.text: Contiguous memory layout gives strong cache locality for the by-far-most-common operation (iteration/random access), and amortized O(1) append is cheap enough that most 'frequent insertion' workloads don't actually need a linked list's O(1) worst-case insert-anywhere.
Linked lists cannot support insertion in less than O(n) time in any language.
Arrays use less total memory than linked lists in every case.
It's purely historical convention with no remaining technical justification.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's central judgment call: real workloads read/iterate far more than they insert-in-the-middle, and cache locality dominates at realistic sizes — so the general-purpose default optimizes for the common case, while linked lists remain the right tool for specific patterns (frequent middle-insertion/deletion with iterators already positioned, as in some queue/LRU implementations). (b) linked lists genuinely do offer O(1) insert given a reference to the node — that's not the issue. (c) arrays can over-allocate (spare capacity) and linked lists pay per-node pointer overhead — neither always wins on memory. (d) there's a concrete, current technical reason (cache behavior), not just convention.
